talk_title: BIGHAT-299 first-in-class CCAM6-directed TCE for solid malignancies

targets.dependency_context: high CCAM6 expression on solid tumor cells vs neutrophils

models.name: primary neutrophils and cancer cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: CCAM6 high expression in tumor cells, 28k copies on neutrophils

models.name: PDX panel (12-15 models)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Diverse solid tumor histologies with CCAM6 expression

models.name: CCAM6 humanized CD34+ mouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: human CCAM6 expression on myeloid cells

MOA_summary: BIGHAT-299 is a conditionally active bispecific T‑cell engager that binds a low‑affinity VHH to CCAM6 on tumor cells and a pH‑conditional CD3 arm, achieving selective cytotoxicity against high‑density CCAM6 tumor cells while sparing neutrophils, leading to potent tumor regression in preclinical models.
key_findings: BIGHAT-299 exhibits a 1,000‑fold IC50 differential between CCAM6‑high tumor cells and neutrophils (20‑100 nM vs 1‑10 pM).
In CCAM6‑humanized mice, weekly dosing up to 2 mg/kg showed no neutrophil depletion or anemia, unlike high‑affinity controls.
Complete tumor regressions were observed in multiple CCAM6‑positive PDX models at 50 µg/kg weekly.
Pharmacokinetic studies in cynomolgus monkeys demonstrated multi‑day half‑life and sub‑Q bioavailability >120 mg/mL.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity achieved by exploiting >80‑fold antigen density difference between tumor and normal neutrophils.
No activity observed against monocytes/macrophages with 3‑10 k copies per cell.
open_questions: What is the minimal CCAM6 copy number required for therapeutic efficacy in heterogeneous patient tumors?
Can the conditional CD3 affinity be further tuned to improve safety in humans?

talk_title: ENA-101 first-in-class bispecific T‑cell engager targeting DART‑fox peptide‑HLA A3

targets.dependency_context: HLA‑A3 positive tumors expressing DART‑fox peptide

biomarkers.name: HLA‑A3 positivity
biomarkers.finding: 20‑30% of patients in US/EU are HLA‑A3 positive, enabling enrollment

biomarkers.name: DART‑fox A3 peptide expression
biomarkers.finding: RNA‑ISH score ≥2 in >70% of lung squamous, TNBC, head‑and‑neck samples

models.name: DART‑fox positive cancer lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: HLA‑A3 positive, endogenous DART‑fox peptide presentation

models.name: CRISPR DART‑fox knockout colorectal line
models.genotype_or_key_features: no DART‑fox peptide, serves as negative control

models.name: NSG mouse tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: human tumor cells + human PBMCs, doses 50 µg/kg‑1 mg/kg

MOA_summary: ENA‑101 is a bispecific T‑cell engager that binds a high‑affinity antibody specific for the DART‑fox A3 peptide presented by HLA‑A3 and an intermediate‑affinity anti‑CD3 scFv, delivering potent, peptide‑specific T‑cell activation and tumor cell killing while sparing normal HLA‑A3 positive tissues.
key_findings: ENA‑101 binds DART‑fox A3/HLA‑A3 with double‑digit picomolar affinity and shows no off‑target binding to HLA‑A3 alone.
In vitro cytotoxicity reaches sub‑10 pM EC50 against DART‑fox positive lines, with no activity against knockout controls.
No cytokine release syndrome signals observed in whole‑blood assays up to high concentrations.
In NSG xenograft models, weekly dosing (50 µg/kg‑1 mg/kg) achieved complete tumor regressions without detectable toxicity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity driven by peptide‑HLA presentation; loss of HLA‑A3 or peptide expression could confer resistance.
HLA‑A3 loss of heterozygosity occurs in ~7‑10% of patients, representing a potential escape mechanism.
open_questions: Can ENA‑101 synergize with checkpoint inhibitors in HLA‑A3 positive patients?
What is the durability of T‑cell memory responses after peptide‑specific killing?

talk_title: NRP1‑targeted peptide‑linked antibodies redirect viral memory T cells

biomarkers.name: NRP1 expression
biomarkers.finding: High NRP1 IHC in >70% of tested tumor lines

models.name: NSG tumor xenograft with human PBMCs
HLA‑A2 transgenic mouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: systemic expression of HLA‑A2 for safety assessment

MOA_summary: A NRP1‑binding antibody (5D6) chemically or recombinantly linked to viral peptides (e.g., CMV NLV) is internalized, processed, and cross‑presented on HLA‑A2, enabling circulating virus‑specific T cells to recognize and kill NRP1‑positive tumor cells while sparing normal NRP1‑expressing tissues.
key_findings: 5D6‑NLV delivers >200 copies of CMV peptide per tumor cell, sufficient for TCR‑mediated killing.
In vitro, peptide‑linked 5D6 induces dose‑dependent IFN‑γ and TNF‑α release from CMV‑specific T cells.
In NSG xenograft models, a single dose of peptide‑linked 5D6 plus 5 × 10⁶ TCR‑T cells eradicated tumors without off‑target toxicity.
No cytokine release or cytotoxicity observed against NRP1‑positive endothelial or hematopoietic cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential resistance if tumor down‑regulates NRP1 or HLA‑A2.
Cross‑presentation efficiency varies with antigen density; low NRP1 may limit peptide loading.
open_questions: Can this platform be extended to other viral or tumor‑derived peptides?
What is the durability of redirected viral memory T‑cell responses in vivo?

talk_title: PrimeBody platform enables tumor‑activated CD47 targeting with superior therapeutic index

targets.dependency_context: ubiquitous CD47 expression; tumor activation via protease‑cleavable linker

biomarkers.name: CD47 expression
biomarkers.finding: High CD47 on all solid tumors evaluated

models.name: CD47‑humanized + SIRPα‑humanized mouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: allows assessment of anemia and PK

MOA_summary: VORA101 is a high‑affinity SIRPα‑based CD47 blocker fused to an enhanced Fc and masked with a protease‑cleavable linker; tumor‑specific proteases remove the mask, restoring CD47 blockade and Fc‑mediated ADCP/ADCC while sparing normal tissues, thereby eliminating anemia and improving exposure.
key_findings: Masked VORA101 shows >500‑fold tumor‑selective activation in ex vivo human tumor tissue versus serum.
In CD47‑humanized mice, VORA101 prevented anemia at doses where unmasked migrolimab caused severe anemia.
VORA101 achieved 2‑fold higher serum concentrations than migrolimab due to reduced tissue sink.
In Raji xenografts, masked VORA101 induced complete tumor regressions, outperforming migrolimab.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Efficacy depends on presence of tumor‑associated proteases; protease down‑regulation could reduce activation.
Mask designed for human proteases; mouse models show lower off‑target cleavage.

combination_strategies.rationale: CD47 blockade synergizes with Fc‑active HER2 antibody to promote ADCP
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved ORR in HER2‑positive gastric model

open_questions: Which protease signatures best predict activation across the selected indications?
Can the mask be further optimized to reduce any residual peripheral activation?

talk_title: Integrated CD2 costimulation in trispecific T‑cell engagers improves T‑cell fitness

targets.dependency_context: CD3 on T cells; CD2 costimulation via CD58 on tumor

biomarkers.name: CD2 expression
biomarkers.finding: Detected on >80% of tumor‑infiltrating T cells across scRNA‑seq datasets

MOA_summary: Evolve‑104 is a trispecific engager that combines anti‑CD3, tumor antigen binding, and a CD2 agonist (via CD58 engagement), delivering signal‑1 and signal‑2 simultaneously to T cells, resulting in enhanced proliferation, reduced exhaustion, and superior antitumor activity.
key_findings: CD2‑integrated Evolve‑104 induced 2‑fold higher CD8 proliferation and Ki67 positivity at day 14 versus conventional bispecifics.
Affinity‑tuned Evolve‑104 achieved ~60% tumor cell killing at 1:10 E:T ratio, outperforming bispecifics.
In NCI‑H292 xenografts, Evolve‑104 produced ~60% tumor growth inhibition, whereas bispecifics achieved ~40%.
Ex vivo TIL assays showed the greatest up‑regulation of CD25 and CD137 with affinity‑tuned Evolve‑104.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential for increased CRS mitigated by CD3 affinity tuning; preclinical assays show reduced cytokine release.
open_questions: Will CD2 costimulation affect NK‑cell activity in patients?
What is the optimal CD3 affinity to balance efficacy and safety in humans?

talk_title: Preclinical evaluation and safety of CVX250, a bispecific T‑cell engager targeting Cathepsin G peptide‑HLA in AML

speaker.name: Jennifer Hibel
speaker.affiliation: Crossbow Therapeutics / MD Anderson Cancer Center

targets.dependency_context: HLA‑A2 positive AML cells presenting CG1 peptide

biomarkers.name: HLA‑A2 positivity
biomarkers.finding: Present in ~40% of US population; required for enrollment

biomarkers.name: CG1 peptide copy number
biomarkers.finding: Potent killing observed down to ~33 copies per cell

models.name: NSG mouse with human PBMCs and ML2 AML

MOA_summary: CVX250 is a TCR‑mimetic bispecific that binds the CG1 peptide presented by HLA‑A2 on AML cells and CD3 on T cells, mediating picomolar‑potent cytotoxicity and bystander killing of CG1‑negative AML cells via cytokine‑mediated up‑regulation of death receptors.
key_findings: CVX250 kills AML cell lines with EC50 values in the single‑digit picomolar range, even at 33 CG1 copies per cell.
Bystander killing observed when target‑positive cells were mixed with target‑negative cells, indicating cytokine‑mediated effects.
No significant cytokine release or platelet activation detected in whole‑blood assays up to high concentrations.
PK in cynomolgus monkeys showed IgG‑like half‑life (7‑14 days) with minimal immunogenicity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential resistance if AML down‑regulates HLA‑A2 or CG1 processing.
FcγR2A binding observed at high concentrations but unlikely to be functional at therapeutic levels.
open_questions: Will CVX250 retain activity in the bone‑marrow microenvironment where HLA expression may be lower?
Can combination with hypomethylating agents increase CG1 presentation?

talk_title: First‑in‑class monoclonal antibody targeting TM4SF5 for gastrointestinal cancers

biomarkers.name: TM4SF5 expression
biomarkers.finding: High TM4SF5 correlates with ~2‑fold worse overall survival in HCC and CRC

models.name: CRC patient‑derived xenograft
models.genotype_or_key_features: Variable response; up to 60% TGI in responders

MOA_summary: RCT1213 binds a conformational epitope spanning both extracellular loops of TM4SF5, disrupting its interaction with IL‑6R/STAT3 and SLAMF7, thereby reducing immunosuppressive cytokine signaling, restoring NK‑cell recognition, and reshaping the tumor microenvironment toward immune activation.
key_findings: RCT1213 binds EC1/EC2 loops with sub‑nanomolar affinity and blocks TM4SF5‑IL‑6R interaction, reducing STAT3 phosphorylation.
In HCC mouse models, RCT1213 achieved ~80% tumor growth inhibition, comparable to atezolizumab + bevacizumab.
Combination of RCT1213 with atezolizumab and bevacizumab yielded complete responses in all tested mice.
In CRC PDX, dose‑dependent tumor suppression observed; responders showed increased CD8⁺ T cells and decreased Tregs.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential resistance if tumor down‑regulates TM4SF5 or compensates via alternative tetraspanins.
Selectivity confirmed by minimal off‑target binding in biodistribution studies.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade may synergize with TM4SF5‑mediated TME remodeling
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete tumor regression in HCC models

combination_strategies.rationale: Anti‑angiogenic effect complements TM4SF5‑driven STAT3 inhibition

open_questions: Will TM4SF5 expression serve as a reliable predictive biomarker across diverse GI tumor subtypes?
Can RCT1213 be engineered into a bispecific format to further enhance efficacy?
